text:
Hello, everyone!
A few weeks before I came here, I was thinking I was working on things nobody cares about. Now I call them _Explorable Explanations / Media for Thinking the Unthinkable_, and I feel better (a bit) :slightly_smiling_face:. At least there’s a chance for someone to ask questions or join in. Here are some examples:
• *<https://learn-together.co/en/dbook/|YMO>* (translate("I love my textbooks", { to: "bg" }).split(" ").map(word => word[0]).reverse().join("").toUpperCase();) is an e-ink app for learning from PDFs. You can extend the e-paper anywhere, adding blank space to fill with handwritten notes or an interactive (web) component. This allows <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=73CXXhrwWqQ|learning by reading (actively), drawing, exploring, making, and co-authoring> – all in one place. Interesting here is what happens when components interact with the PDF content.
• A block-based (Snap!) <https://snap.berkeley.edu/snap/snap.html#present:Username=tsvetelin&ProjectName=Composer%20Vivaldi%20Spring&editMode|interface for music composition>. Blocks are notes, tunes are scripts, and vice-blocks (C-shaped) are composers’ tools that let you manipulate tunes. Similar to what <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0WX_gokLiJA|Bach>, <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TQEpknjUeZg|Rachmaninoff>, <https://www.youtube.com/shorts/AlSB7PCU2tM|Ritchie Blackmore>, etc. once did. They didn’t use this interface, of course, but working on it inspired me to create two pieces of music, <https://youtu.be/Kv70dsVTfw4|Congruences: Swan Lake in the Rhodopes. Spring> (original sound with my bagpipe coming soon), a <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2lcs26Bo5b0|Rhodopean fugue> (a fugue-like 4+ voices piece based on a traditional tune)
• <https://sites.google.com/view/igromat?usp=sharing|Igromat> is a board game generator set consisting of customizable tiles, magnet board, a few types of dice and pieces. One can arrange it to play Chess, Go, Ur, etc; create a board game to fit special occasions; explore how changing the math model underneath reflects the game play.
• Video-like presentations of the joy of programming where you can pause the lesson and interact with the current state of the program being demonstrated. The work is based on <https://csc.ncsu.edu/people/twprice/|Thomas Price>’s Snap! replay. There’s a demo (in Bulgarian) for <https://learn-together.co/lessons-loom/playback.html?video=learntogether/loom|weaving on a virtual loom> and <https://learn-together.co/lessons-composer/playback.html?video=learntogether/composer|composing music>.
So what is the first thought when you read those? What ideas do you get? How do you relate this with your experience?
Besides that most of my time goes into developing software. Occasionally, I assist in workshops for teachers and students called _<https://docs.google.com/document/d/1rGSAKNZDV5N7683mfod_pEeXA9Z6cSJAaEkKSQ-uMJc/edit?usp=sharing|Mathematics as Music for the Soul>_ and play traditional music with my <https://www.kabagaida.com/band.html|band>. I’m moving to Switzerland soon, so any meetups in between there and Bulgaria are welcomed.

text:
Hello both! This might look like I’m putting my nose into other people’s business.
I started browsing around to get oriented and noticed that GToolkit is a thing here.

Here is a basic question, as per Tudor’s encouragement:
Does it make sense to import the system I’m currently working on and have the toolkit generate multiple representations of it? For example, could I just import the three folders of my current system (three repos for FE, BE, and ML) and start exploring?

While watching the presentation of the Kit, I began thinking about how the legacy-system migration approach could be applied to our education system. It seems like a legacy, outdated system—not only here in Bulgaria, but everywhere. So perhaps by understanding it deeply and creating thousands of tools around it, we could find a way to migrate it.
